# MCP Tool Poisoning via Prompt Injection (Confused Deputy)

**Platform:** ai-agent · **Category:** prompt-injection · **Severity:** critical

Injected instructions in untrusted content cause an LLM agent to invoke high-privilege MCP tools — a confused-deputy escalation that requires no credentials.

**MITRE ATT&CK:** [T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/)
## Description

An LLM agent acts as a confused deputy when it ingests content from outside its trust boundary — a fetched webpage, a retrieved document, a prior tool result — and that content contains instructions the model interprets as coming from the user. The attacker does not need any credentials or direct access to the agent; they only need to place a payload in a source the agent will read. Once ingested, the injected instruction directs the agent to call a high-privilege tool (send mail, write files, exfiltrate data, execute code) with the agent's own session credentials. The OS analogue is exact: a low-trust principal (untrusted content) exploits a gap between the permission the system intends to grant and the permission it actually enforces — the difference being that here the "kernel" is the LLM and the "SUID bit" is the agent's unconditional willingness to execute whatever it decides to. The `atlas.policy.PolicyEngine` in `src/atlas/policy.py` is the reference control: it gates every tool call by the provenance of its trigger, so that an `UNTRUSTED`-provenance call to a tool with `min_trust=USER` is denied or held for human confirmation regardless of what the model decided to do.

## Requirements

- An LLM agent with access to at least one high-privilege MCP tool
- The agent processes content from an untrusted source (web fetch, file read, search result, prior tool output)
- No provenance-aware policy engine gates tool calls by trigger source
## Exploitation

1. Identify an agent that reads from untrusted sources and has access to high-impact tools (email, file write, code exec, API calls).
2. Craft a prompt-injection payload and plant it where the agent will ingest it (a target webpage, an uploaded document, a search-result snippet).
3. The agent fetches or reads the source; the injected instruction overrides or extends the agent's task.
4. The agent calls the targeted high-privilege tool using its own credentials, believing it is following legitimate user intent.
## Proof of Concept

- [`poc/demo.py`](./poc/demo.py)
## Detection

| Type | Artifact | Description |
|------|----------|--------------|
| sigma | [`detections/sigma/agent_toolcall_escalation.yml`](./detections/sigma/agent_toolcall_escalation.yml) | Flags tool calls with untrusted provenance in the atlas PolicyEngine audit log (JSON lines). |

## Mitigation

- Deploy a provenance-aware policy engine (see src/atlas/policy.py) — every tool call must carry the trust level of whatever triggered it, and high-privilege tools must require USER or TRUSTED provenance.
- Never allow untrusted content to auto-trigger destructive or irreversible tool calls; require explicit human confirmation for any tool with min_trust > UNTRUSTED.
- Treat agent tool calls as an audit surface — log every call with session_id, tool_name, provenance, and decision in a tamper-evident store.
- Sandbox agent tool permissions by session context; a "research agent" session should not have write or send-mail capabilities even if the underlying tool exists.
## References

- [MITRE ATT&CK T1059](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/)
- [ATLAS AML.T0051 — LLM Prompt Injection](https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0051)

- [Anthropic: Prompt Injection in Agentic Contexts](https://www.anthropic.com/research/claude-character)

> For authorized AI security testing and research only. demo.py does not make any real tool calls; it simulates the policy engine decision logic.
